news_summary: Recent space advancements showcase groundbreaking discoveries of unusual exoplanets, cutting-edge Mars helicopter technology, enhanced lunar mission preparations, and expanding international collaboration through the Artemis Accords.
news_summary_fr: Les récentes avancées spatiales mettent en lumière des découvertes révolutionnaires concernant des exoplanètes inhabituelles, une technologie de pointe en matière d'hélicoptères martiens, des préparatifs renforcés pour les missions lunaires et une collaboration internationale croissante dans le cadre des Accords Artémis.
news_summary_es: Los recientes avances espaciales ponen de manifiesto descubrimientos revolucionarios de exoplanetas inusuales, tecnología de vanguardia en helicópteros marcianos, preparativos mejorados para las misiones lunares y una colaboración internacional cada vez mayor a través de los Acuerdos Artemis.

articles.title: This strange planet pair shouldn’t exist, but it does
articles.summary: A bizarre planetary pairing 190 light-years away is challenging everything astronomers thought they knew about how worlds form. A “lonely” hot Jupiter — typically found without nearby companions — is sharing its system with a smaller mini-Neptune tucked even closer to the star, a setup once thought nearly impossible.
articles.content:
Across the Milky Way, about 190 light years from Earth, astronomers have identified a highly unusual planetary pairing. A massive hot Jupiter, a type of giant planet typically found alone, shares its system with a smaller mini-Neptune orbiting even closer to their star. This rare configuration has puzzled scientists since it was first discovered in 2020.

Now, researchers at MIT have taken a closer look at the inner planet's atmosphere and uncovered new clues that help explain how this strange system formed.

JWST Reveals a Heavy, Water-Rich Atmosphere

In a study published in Astrophysical Journal Letters, the team used NASA's James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) to analyze the atmosphere of the mini-Neptune. This marks the first time scientists have measured the atmospheric composition of a mini-Neptune located inside the orbit of a hot Jupiter.

The observations show that the planet's atmosphere is surprisingly dense and filled with heavier molecules, including water vapor, carbon dioxide, sulfur dioxide, and traces of methane. This type of atmosphere would be unlikely if the planet had formed close to its star, where lighter gases usually dominate.

Instead, the findings suggest a very different origin.

Planets Likely Formed Far From Their Star

According to the researchers, both the mini-Neptune and the hot Jupiter probably formed much farther away from their star, in a colder region of the system's early disk of gas and dust. In that environment, icy material and volatile compounds could accumulate more easily, allowing the planets to build thicker, heavier atmospheres.

Over time, the two planets likely migrated inward together, moving closer to their star while maintaining their atmospheres and their unusual orbital arrangement.

The results provide the first clear evidence that mini-Neptunes can form beyond a star's "frost line," the distance at which temperatures are low enough for water to freeze into ice.

"This is the first time we've observed the atmosphere of a planet that is inside the orbit of a hot Jupiter," says Saugata Barat, a postdoc in MIT's Kavli Institute for Astrophysics and Space Research and the lead author of the study. "This measurement tells us this mini-Neptune indeed formed beyond the frost line, giving confirmation that this formation channel does exist."

The research team includes scientists from institutions around the world, including MIT, the Harvard and Smithsonian Center for Astrophysics, the University of South Queensland, the University of Texas at Austin, and Lund University.

A Rare and Puzzling Planetary System

Mini-Neptunes are smaller than Neptune and are made mostly of gas surrounding a rocky core. They are actually the most common type of planet found in the Milky Way, even though none exist in our own solar system.

In 2020, Chelsea X. Huang, then a Torres Postdoctoral fellow at MIT (now on the faculty at University of South Queensland), identified this unusual system. The mini-Neptune was found orbiting alongside a hot Jupiter, something astronomers rarely see.

Using data from NASA's Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite (TESS), the team studied a star called TOI-1130 and detected both planets. The mini-Neptune completes an orbit every four days, while the hot Jupiter takes eight days.

"This was a one-of-a-kind system," says Huang. "Hot Jupiters are 'lonely,' meaning they don't have companion planets inside their orbits. They are so massive, and their gravity is so strong, that whatever is inside their orbit just gets scattered away. But somehow, with this hot Jupiter, an inner companion has survived. And that raises questions about how such a system could form."

Timing the Observation Was a Challenge

The discovery led researchers to investigate the planets in more detail using JWST, focusing on the inner world known as TOI-1130b.

However, observing the planet was not straightforward. Unlike most planets, which follow predictable orbital schedules, this pair is in what scientists call "mean motion resonance." Each planet's gravity slightly alters the other's orbit, making their movements less regular and harder to predict.

To overcome this, a team led by Judith Korth of Lund University compiled previous observations and created a model to determine exactly when the planets would pass in front of their star in a way JWST could observe.

"It was a challenging prediction, and we had to be spot-on," Barat says.

A Detailed Look at Planetary Chemistry

Once the timing was right, JWST captured detailed data across multiple wavelengths of light.

"The beauty of JWST is that it does not observe just in one color, but at different colors, or wavelengths," Barat explains. "And the specific wavelengths that a planet absorbs can tell you a lot about the composition of its atmosphere."

The data revealed strong signatures of water, carbon dioxide, and sulfur dioxide, along with smaller amounts of methane. These heavier molecules contrast with the lighter hydrogen and helium typically expected in planets that form close to their stars.

This finding challenges previous assumptions and supports the idea that TOI-1130b formed much farther out before migrating inward.

Evidence for Planetary Migration

The planet likely gathered its atmosphere in a cold region beyond the frost line, where water freezes onto dust grains and forms icy particles. As the young planet moved inward, the ice would have evaporated, leaving behind the thick atmosphere seen today.

Barat says the presence of these heavy molecules confirms that both planets likely originated in the outer regions of their system and migrated inward together while preserving their atmospheres.

"This system represents one of the rarest architectures that astronomers have ever found," Barat says. "The observations of TOI-1130b provide the first hint that such mini-Neptunes that form beyond the water/ice line are indeed present in nature."

This work was supported, in part, by NASA.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery using the James Webb Space Telescope that challenges existing theories of planetary formation, providing new insights into how planets form and migrate. This breakthrough has broad implications for astronomy and our understanding of planetary systems beyond our own, representing a meaningful advancement in space science.
articles.personality_title: Strange planet pair challenges ideas about how worlds form
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – About 190 light-years from Earth, astronomers found a strange pair of planets orbiting a star called TOI-1130. Usually, a big planet called a hot Jupiter is alone, but here it shares its system with a smaller planet called a mini-Neptune, orbiting even closer to the star.

**What happened** – Using NASA’s James Webb Space Telescope (JWST), scientists studied the mini-Neptune’s atmosphere for the first time inside a hot Jupiter’s orbit. They discovered the mini-Neptune’s atmosphere is heavy and full of water vapor, carbon dioxide, sulfur dioxide, and methane. This means the planet likely formed far from its star, where it was cold enough for ice, and then moved closer over time.

**Impact** – This discovery changes what we thought about how planets form and move. It shows that mini-Neptunes can start far from their stars, beyond a cold area called the frost line, and travel inward while keeping their thick atmospheres. Also, it’s rare to find a hot Jupiter with a nearby smaller planet, so this system is unusual and helps scientists learn more about different planet setups.

**What's next step** – Researchers will keep studying systems like TOI-1130 to understand how common these unusual planet pairs are. They might also use JWST and other telescopes to look for more mini-Neptunes inside hot Jupiter orbits and learn about their atmospheres and histories.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists used the James Webb Space Telescope to find that a rare pair of planets formed far from their star and moved inward together, changing how we understand planet formation and movement.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une paire étrange de planètes remet en cause nos idées sur la formation des mondes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – À environ 190 années-lumière de la Terre, des astronomes ont découvert une paire étrange de planètes autour d’une étoile appelée TOI-1130. Habituellement, une grosse planète appelée Jupiter chaude est seule, mais ici elle partage son système avec une plus petite planète, une mini-Neptune, qui orbite encore plus près de l’étoile.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Avec le télescope spatial James Webb (JWST) de la NASA, les scientifiques ont étudié pour la première fois l’atmosphère de la mini-Neptune située à l’intérieur de l’orbite de la Jupiter chaude. Ils ont découvert que son atmosphère est dense et contient beaucoup de vapeur d’eau, de dioxyde de carbone, de dioxyde de soufre et de méthane. Cela montre que cette planète s’est probablement formée loin de son étoile, dans une région froide où la glace pouvait se former, avant de se rapprocher.

**Impact** – Cette découverte change notre compréhension de la formation et du déplacement des planètes. Elle prouve que les mini-Neptunes peuvent se former au-delà d’une zone froide appelée ligne de gel, puis migrer vers l’intérieur en gardant leur atmosphère épaisse. De plus, il est rare de trouver une Jupiter chaude avec une planète plus petite proche, ce qui rend ce système unique et précieux pour les études.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à étudier des systèmes comme TOI-1130 pour voir si ce type de paire de planètes est courant. Ils utiliseront aussi JWST et d’autres télescopes pour observer plus de mini-Neptunes proches de Jupiter chaudes et mieux comprendre leurs atmosphères et leur histoire.

**En une phrase** – Grâce au télescope James Webb, les scientifiques ont découvert qu’une paire rare de planètes s’est formée loin de son étoile puis a migré ensemble vers l’intérieur, modifiant notre vision de la formation et du déplacement planétaires.
articles.personality_title_es: Pareja extraña de planetas desafía ideas sobre cómo se forman los mundos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – A unos 190 años luz de la Tierra, los astrónomos encontraron una pareja rara de planetas que orbitan una estrella llamada TOI-1130. Normalmente, un planeta grande llamado Júpiter caliente está solo, pero aquí comparte su sistema con un planeta más pequeño llamado mini-Neptuno que orbita aún más cerca de la estrella.

**Qué pasó** – Usando el telescopio espacial James Webb (JWST) de la NASA, los científicos estudiaron por primera vez la atmósfera del mini-Neptuno dentro de la órbita del Júpiter caliente. Descubrieron que la atmósfera es densa y contiene vapor de agua, dióxido de carbono, dióxido de azufre y metano. Esto sugiere que el planeta se formó lejos de su estrella, donde hacía frío y se podía formar hielo, y luego se movió hacia adentro.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo cambia lo que pensábamos sobre cómo se forman y mueven los planetas. Muestra que los mini-Neptunos pueden formarse más allá de una zona fría llamada línea de escarcha y luego migrar hacia el interior manteniendo su atmósfera gruesa. Además, es raro encontrar un Júpiter caliente con un planeta pequeño cerca, por lo que este sistema es especial y ayuda a entender diferentes tipos de sistemas planetarios.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán estudiando sistemas como TOI-1130 para ver qué tan comunes son estas parejas de planetas. También usarán JWST y otros telescopios para buscar más mini-Neptunos dentro de las órbitas de Júpiter calientes y aprender más sobre sus atmósferas e historias.

**Una frase clave** – Científicos usaron el telescopio James Webb para descubrir que una pareja rara de planetas se formó lejos de su estrella y migró hacia adentro junta, cambiando nuestra comprensión de la formación y movimiento planetarios.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of two distinct planets orbiting a glowing star: a large, fiery hot Jupiter with swirling red and orange clouds, and a smaller, cooler mini-Neptune nearby with a dense, misty atmosphere rich in blues and whites, connected by subtle glowing trails symbolizing their close, unusual orbital dance and shared origin far from their star.

articles.title: NASA Pushes Next-Gen Mars Helicopter Rotor Blades Past Mach 1
articles.summary: The rotor blades that will carry NASA’s next-generation helicopters to new Martian heights broke the sound barrier during March tests at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California. Data from the tests, which took place in a special chamber that can simulate environmental conditions on the Red Planet, indicate that the fastest traveling part of […]
articles.content:
Engineer Jaakko Karras inspects a next-generation Mars helicopter rotor blade prior to testing it at supersonic speeds in the 25-Foot Space Simulator at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in November 2025. NASA/JPL-Caltech Inside the dark chamber of JPL’s 25-Foot Space Simulator, an engineer examines a test stand used to investigate the performance of next-generation Mars helicopter rotor blades at high speeds. The image was taken in November 2025. NASA/JPL-Caltech

The rotor blades that will carry NASA’s next-generation helicopters to new Martian heights broke the sound barrier during March tests at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California. Data from the tests, which took place in a special chamber that can simulate environmental conditions on the Red Planet, indicate that the fastest traveling part of the rotor blade, the tips, can be accelerated beyond Mach 1 without breaking apart. Data gathered from 137 test runs will enable engineers to design aircraft capable of carrying heavier payloads, including science instruments.

“NASA had a great run with the Ingenuity Mars Helicopter, but we are asking these next-generation aircraft to do even more at the Red Planet,” said Al Chen, Mars Exploration Program manager at JPL. “That’s not an easy ask. While everything about Mars is hard, flying there is just about the hardest thing you can do. That’s because its atmosphere is so incredibly thin that it is hard to generate lift, and yet Mars has significant gravity.”

By pushing rotors beyond the speed of sound during recent testing at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory, engineers are unlocking new possibilities for low-altitude aerial exploration of Mars. Credit: NASA/JPL-Caltech

Ingenuity, which performed the first powered, controlled flight on another world just over five years ago on April 19, 2021, was a trailblazing technology demonstration that did not carry science instruments. The agency’s recently announced SkyFall project and other potential future Mars aircraft will be capable of carrying payloads — including science instruments and sensors — to collect data in support of future human and robotic missions, leveraging the advantages that come with low-altitude aerial exploration.

Need for speed

In the fast-moving world of rotors, more thrust comes from a quicker spin or a larger diameter. Although this axiom holds true on Earth, engineers designing aircraft for the Red Planet must be much more aggressive. Because the Mars atmosphere is only 1% as dense as Earth’s, maximizing thrust requires pushing blade tips toward the speed of sound to achieve significant lift. While small-diameter rotors on Earth can also rotate at thousands of revolutions per minute, they have more air molecules to push and don’t need to approach the sonic edge.

To view this video please enable JavaScript, and consider upgrading to a web browser that supports HTML5 video NASA’s Ingenuity Mars Helicopter does a slow spin test of its blades on April 8, 2021, the 48th Martian day, or sol, of the mission. The rotorcraft, captured here by the Mastcam-Z instrument aboard NASA’s Perseverance rover, completed its historic first flight less than two Earth weeks later. NASA/JPL-Caltech/ASU/MSSS

The Ingenuity flight team never allowed the rotational speed of their composite-skinned foam rotors to exceed 2,700 rpm during the helicopter’s 72 flights at Mars for two reasons: to avoid the unpredictable physics of the sound barrier and to make sure that an unexpected gust of wind (from a dust devil, for instance) wouldn’t send the rotor tips over the sonic edge.

“If Chuck Yeager were here, he’d tell you things can get squirrely around Mach 1,” said JPL’s Jaakko Karras, the rotor test lead. “With that in mind, we planned Ingenuity’s flights to keep the rotor blade tips at Mach 0.7 with no wind so that if we encountered a Martian headwind while in flight, the rotor tips wouldn’t go supersonic. But we want more performance from our next-gen Mars aircraft. We needed to know that our rotors could go faster safely.”

While Mach 1 on Earth at sea level is approximately 760 mph (1,223 kph), the speed of sound on Mars is significantly slower — roughly 540 mph (869 kph) — due to the planet’s thin, cold, carbon-dioxide-rich atmosphere.

Blade-proof chamber

To begin evaluating the rotors, which were developed and manufactured by AeroVironment in Simi Valley, California, Karras and his team mounted a three-bladed rotor that could be used in future Mars helicopter designs inside the historic 25-Foot Space Simulator at JPL. They evacuated the air and replaced it with just enough carbon dioxide to match the Martian atmosphere, then blasted the rotor with wind as it spun at increasing speeds.

The test engineers had taken the precaution of lining part of the chamber with sheet metal in case the blades broke apart during the supersonic experiment. From a control room a few yards away from the chamber, the team watched displays showing data and a view inside the chamber as the rpm climbed as high as 3,750. At that rate, the tips were traveling at Mach 0.98. Then the engineers activated a fan inside the chamber that pelted the rotors with headwinds. After each run, they increased in wind velocity for the next run.

The team pushed rotor tip speeds to Mach 1.08, boosting the Mars vehicle’s lift capability by 30%. This breakthrough allows future missions to support heavier scientific payloads, including advanced sensors and larger batteries for extended flight.

Next the team tried their luck with the two-bladed SkyFall rotor. Because it is slightly longer than the three-bladed version, only 3,570 rpm was needed to achieve the same near-supersonic speed at the rotor tips prior to introducing the headwinds.

“The successful testing of these rotors was a major step toward proving the feasibility of flight in more demanding environments, which is key for next-gen vehicles,” said Shannah Withrow-Maser, an aerodynamicist from NASA’s Ames Research Center in Silicon Valley and member of the test team. “We thought we’d be lucky to hit Mach 1.05, and we reached Mach 1.08 on our last runs. We’re still digging into the data, and there may be even more thrust on the table. These next-gen helicopters are going to be amazing.”

The SkyFall mission design team has incorporated the test team’s findings into the performance specifications. Inspired by Ingenuity, the only rotorcraft to fly on another planet to date, SkyFall is designed to carry three next-gen Mars helicopters to the Red Planet in December 2028.

More about NASA’s Mars Exploration Program

The faster-than-sound spin test campaign was funded by the agency’s Mars Exploration Program in pursuit of maximizing the capability of future aircraft flying at the Red Planet. A division of Caltech in Pasadena, JPL manages the Mars Exploration Program for NASA’s Science Mission Directorate in Washington.

For more information about NASA’s Mars Exploration Program, visit:

https://mars.nasa.gov

Media Contacts

DC Agle

Jet Propulsion Laboratory, Pasadena, Calif.

818-393-9011

agle@jpl.nasa.gov

Karen Fox / Alana Johnson

NASA Headquarters, Washington

240-285-5155 / 202-672-4780

karen.c.fox@nasa.gov / alana.r.johnson@nasa.gov

2026-029
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological breakthrough by NASA in developing next-generation Mars helicopter rotor blades that can safely exceed the speed of sound in the Martian atmosphere. This advancement enables future Mars aircraft to carry heavier scientific payloads, enhancing the scope and capability of Mars exploration missions. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a real-world achievement with broad implications for space exploration and scientific discovery.
articles.personality_title: NASA tests Mars helicopter blades that spin faster than sound
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA has been exploring Mars using helicopters like Ingenuity, which flew there for the first time in 2021. These helicopters face a big challenge because Mars has a very thin atmosphere, making it hard to fly and lift heavy equipment.

**What happened** – In March 2026, engineers at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory tested new rotor blades for Mars helicopters. They spun the blades inside a special chamber that copies Mars’ air and temperature. The blades reached speeds faster than the speed of sound on Mars, without breaking. This was the first time NASA pushed rotor blades past Mach 1 (the sound barrier) for Mars flight.

**Impact** – This achievement means future Mars helicopters can carry heavier scientific instruments and batteries. The faster spinning blades create 30% more lift, making it possible to explore Mars in more detail from the air. It also shows it’s safe to fly rotors at supersonic speeds on Mars, opening new ways to design stronger, better flying vehicles.

**What's next step** – NASA will use the test data to build and fly next-generation helicopters on Mars, including the SkyFall project planned for 2028. These helicopters will carry more equipment to study Mars and help prepare for future missions with humans and robots.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s successful test of supersonic rotor blades marks a major step toward stronger, more capable Mars helicopters that can carry heavier science tools on the Red Planet.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA teste des pales d’hélicoptère martien dépassant la vitesse du son
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA explore Mars avec des hélicoptères comme Ingenuity, qui a réalisé son premier vol en 2021. Voler sur Mars est difficile car l’atmosphère y est très fine, ce qui complique la portance et le transport de charges lourdes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En mars 2026, des ingénieurs du Jet Propulsion Laboratory de la NASA ont testé de nouvelles pales d’hélicoptère pour Mars. Ils les ont fait tourner dans une chambre spéciale reproduisant l’atmosphère martienne. Les pales ont atteint une vitesse supersonique sans se casser, une première pour un vol martien.

**Impact** – Cette réussite permet aux futurs hélicoptères martiens de transporter des instruments scientifiques plus lourds. Les pales tournant plus vite génèrent 30 % de portance en plus, ouvrant la voie à une exploration aérienne plus approfondie. Elle prouve aussi que voler à vitesse supersonique sur Mars est sûr.

**Prochaine étape** – La NASA utilisera ces données pour construire et envoyer de nouveaux hélicoptères, notamment le projet SkyFall prévu pour 2028. Ces appareils transporteront davantage d’équipements pour étudier Mars et préparer les futures missions humaines et robotiques.

**En une phrase** – Le test réussi de pales supersoniques par la NASA est une avancée majeure pour des hélicoptères martiens plus puissants capables de transporter davantage d’instruments scientifiques.
articles.personality_title_es: La NASA prueba palas de helicóptero marciano que giran más rápido que el sonido
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA ha estado explorando Marte con helicópteros como Ingenuity, que realizó el primer vuelo en 2021. Volar en Marte es difícil porque la atmósfera es muy delgada, lo que dificulta levantar cargas pesadas.

**Qué pasó** – En marzo de 2026, ingenieros del Laboratorio de Propulsión a Chorro de la NASA probaron nuevas palas para helicópteros marcianos. Las hicieron girar dentro de una cámara especial que simula el aire y la temperatura de Marte. Las palas alcanzaron velocidades supersónicas sin romperse, la primera vez que se supera la barrera del sonido para un vuelo en Marte.

**Impacto** – Este logro significa que futuros helicópteros marcianos podrán llevar instrumentos científicos más pesados. Las palas que giran más rápido generan un 30 % más de sustentación, permitiendo explorar Marte desde el aire con más detalle. También demuestra que volar a velocidad supersónica en Marte es seguro.

**Próximo paso** – La NASA usará los datos para construir y enviar helicópteros de nueva generación, incluyendo el proyecto SkyFall previsto para 2028. Estos helicópteros llevarán más equipos para estudiar Marte y ayudarán a preparar misiones futuras con humanos y robots.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La exitosa prueba de palas supersónicas de la NASA es un avance clave para helicópteros marcianos más fuertes y capaces de transportar más instrumentos científicos en el planeta rojo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a futuristic Mars helicopter rotor blade spinning dynamically inside a large, dark cylindrical chamber with subtle hints of red Martian soil and a thin, glowing carbon-dioxide atmosphere swirling around it, symbolizing supersonic speed and advanced aerospace engineering, with abstract, soft light beams representing data and innovation illuminating the scene.

articles.title: Industry Moon Lander Training Cabin Lands at NASA for Artemis
articles.summary: A full-scale mock-up of a crew cabin for a future industry lunar lander for NASA’s Artemis program now is operational for training and testing. The agency and its industry partners will use Blue Origin’s Blue Moon Mark 2 crew cabin for mission simulations as the agency prepares to dock with landers in Earth orbit in […]
articles.content:
Located in Space Vehicle Mockup Facility at NASA’s Johnson Space Center, the full-scale prototype of the crew cabin of Blue Origin’s Blue Moon Mark 2 crew lander is over 15 feet (5 m) tall. NASA

A full-scale mock-up of a crew cabin for a future industry lunar lander for NASA’s Artemis program now is operational for training and testing. The agency and its industry partners will use Blue Origin’s Blue Moon Mark 2 crew cabin for mission simulations as the agency prepares to dock with landers in Earth orbit in 2027 and send astronauts to the Moon by 2028.

NASA is working with two American companies to develop the human landing systems that will safely transport astronauts from lunar orbit to the Moon’s surface and back for Artemis. Blue Origin’s lander, launching uncrewed on top of the company’s New Glenn rocket, will meet astronauts aboard NASA’s Orion spacecraft in lunar orbit. Two astronauts will board the Blue Moon crew lander, which will ferry them to the surface and back to other crew members aboard Orion in lunar orbit following the conclusion of their surface stay.

The Blue Moon crew lander that will fly to the Moon will stand about 52 feet tall. Its crew cabin, located at the base of the lander, will be the living and working space where two astronauts will eat, sleep, conduct science, and observe the lunar environment during their stay.

The prototype at NASA’s Johnson Space Center in Houston is a full-size model, featuring the exterior ladder astronauts will use during their time on the Moon. As NASA and industry teams prepare for future crewed missions to the lunar surface, this model will evolve to support more advanced mission and training needs. Over time, it will become an integrated simulator with interactive systems that help astronauts practice for their flight with ground flight control teams.

NASA and Blue Origin can access the exterior and interior of the crew cabin trainer to conduct a series of human-in-the-loop tests, or tests with human interaction, including mission scenarios, mission control communications, spacesuit checkouts, and preparations for simulated moonwalks. The training cabin will also be used to provide design feedback to the Blue Origin team as the lander continues to be developed and mission planning evolves.

Following the successful Artemis II test flight that took four astronauts around the Moon, NASA will launch the Artemis III mission next year to test critical systems in Earth orbit, including rendezvous and docking with one or both commercial landers from Blue Origin and SpaceX. The agency and its partners will conduct integrated checkouts of life support, communications, propulsion, and potentially new spacesuits. These operations will pave the way for Artemis IV and V in 2028, which will return NASA astronauts to the Moon using these commercial provide landers.

Under Artemis, NASA will send astronauts on increasingly difficult missions to explore more of the Moon for scientific discovery and economic benefits, building the foundation for the first crewed missions to Mars.

Learn more about the Artemis program:

https://www.nasa.gov/artemis
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant and tangible advancement in NASA's Artemis program with the operational full-scale mock-up of Blue Origin's lunar lander crew cabin, which will be used for astronaut training and mission simulations. This development has broad significance for future crewed lunar exploration, representing progress toward returning humans to the Moon and eventually Mars, with clear positive real-world impact and sufficient detail.
articles.personality_title: NASA begins training with full-scale lunar lander cabin for Artemis missions
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA’s Artemis program aims to return astronauts to the Moon by 2028. To prepare, NASA is working with private companies to build new lunar landers that will carry astronauts from lunar orbit to the Moon’s surface and back.

**What happened** – A full-size model of the crew cabin from Blue Origin’s Blue Moon Mark 2 lunar lander has arrived at NASA’s Johnson Space Center. This 15-foot-tall mock-up will be used for astronaut training and mission practice starting now. It includes the living and working space where astronauts will eat, sleep, and conduct science during their Moon missions.

**Impact** – This cabin allows astronauts and NASA teams to practice important tasks like docking with the lander, communicating with mission control, and preparing for moonwalks before the actual missions. It also helps engineers improve the lander design based on real user feedback. This hands-on training tool marks a key step toward safe and successful crewed Moon landings.

**What's next step** – After NASA’s Artemis II mission tests flying around the Moon, Artemis III will test docking with commercial landers like Blue Origin’s in Earth orbit. The crew cabin mock-up will continue evolving into a high-tech simulator to prepare astronauts for these future missions, paving the way for Artemis IV and V to land astronauts on the Moon.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s new full-size lunar lander cabin mock-up is now ready for astronaut training, bringing the Artemis Moon missions closer to reality.
articles.personality_title_fr: La cabine d'entraînement du module lunaire de l'industrie arrive chez NASA pour Artemis
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le programme Artemis de la NASA vise à renvoyer des astronautes sur la Lune d'ici 2028. Pour se préparer, la NASA collabore avec des entreprises privées pour construire de nouveaux modules lunaires qui transporteront les astronautes de l'orbite lunaire à la surface de la Lune et retour.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une maquette grandeur nature de la cabine d'équipage du module lunaire Blue Moon Mark 2 de Blue Origin est arrivée au Johnson Space Center de la NASA. Cette maquette de 5 mètres de haut sera utilisée pour l'entraînement des astronautes et la simulation de missions. Elle comprend l'espace de vie et de travail où les astronautes mangeront, dormiront et feront des expériences pendant leurs missions sur la Lune.

**Impact** – Cette cabine permet aux astronautes et aux équipes de la NASA de s'exercer à des tâches importantes comme l'amarrage avec le module, la communication avec le centre de contrôle et la préparation des sorties lunaires avant les missions réelles. Elle aide aussi les ingénieurs à améliorer le module grâce aux retours des utilisateurs. Cet outil d'entraînement est une étape clé vers des atterrissages lunaires habités sûrs et réussis.

**Prochaine étape** – Après la mission Artemis II qui testera un vol autour de la Lune, Artemis III testera l'amarrage avec des modules commerciaux comme celui de Blue Origin en orbite terrestre. La maquette de la cabine évoluera pour devenir un simulateur avancé afin de préparer les astronautes aux futures missions, ouvrant la voie aux missions Artemis IV et V pour atterrir sur la Lune.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La nouvelle maquette grandeur nature de la cabine du module lunaire de la NASA est prête pour l'entraînement des astronautes, rapprochant les missions Artemis sur la Lune de la réalité.
articles.personality_title_es: La cabina de entrenamiento del módulo lunar industrial llega a la NASA para Artemis
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El programa Artemis de la NASA busca enviar astronautas a la Luna para 2028. Para prepararse, la NASA trabaja con empresas privadas para crear nuevos módulos lunares que transportarán a los astronautas desde la órbita lunar hasta la superficie y de regreso.

**Qué pasó** – Un modelo a tamaño real de la cabina de tripulación del módulo lunar Blue Moon Mark 2 de Blue Origin llegó al Centro Espacial Johnson de la NASA. Este modelo de 5 metros de altura se usará para entrenar a los astronautas y practicar misiones. Incluye el espacio donde los astronautas comerán, dormirán y realizarán experimentos durante su tiempo en la Luna.

**Impacto** – Esta cabina permite que los astronautas y equipos de la NASA practiquen tareas importantes como acoplarse al módulo, comunicarse con el control de misión y prepararse para las caminatas lunares antes de las misiones reales. También ayuda a los ingenieros a mejorar el diseño con base en comentarios reales. Esta herramienta de entrenamiento es un paso clave para lograr aterrizajes lunares tripulados seguros y exitosos.

**Próximo paso** – Después de la misión Artemis II que probará un vuelo alrededor de la Luna, Artemis III probará el acoplamiento con módulos comerciales como el de Blue Origin en órbita terrestre. El modelo de la cabina seguirá evolucionando hasta convertirse en un simulador avanzado para preparar a los astronautas para futuras misiones, allanando el camino para Artemis IV y V que aterrizarán en la Luna.

**Resumen en una frase** – La nueva cabina a tamaño real del módulo lunar de la NASA ya está lista para entrenar astronautas, acercando las misiones Artemis a la Luna a la realidad.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a large, intricately designed lunar lander cabin model inside a spacious training facility, with two astronaut silhouettes preparing near an exterior ladder, bathed in warm, soft lighting that highlights the futuristic yet approachable technology, surrounded by subtle elements suggesting mission control and space exploration readiness.

articles.title: NASA Welcomes Paraguay as 67th Artemis Accords Signatory
articles.summary: The Republic of Paraguay signed the Artemis Accords on Thursday during a ceremony in Asunción, becoming the latest nation to commit to the shared principles guiding civil space exploration. “Today, I am proud to welcome Paraguay as the 67th signatory to the Artemis Accords,” said NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman. “They join an ever-growing coalition of […]
articles.content:
Credit: NASA

The Republic of Paraguay signed the Artemis Accords on Thursday during a ceremony in Asunción, becoming the latest nation to commit to the shared principles guiding civil space exploration.

“Today, I am proud to welcome Paraguay as the 67th signatory to the Artemis Accords,” said NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman. “They join an ever-growing coalition of like-minded nations committed to the peaceful, transparent, and responsible exploration of space. Established by President Trump in his first term, the Artemis Accords provided the principles for how we explore the Moon, Mars, and beyond. Now, with his national space policy, we are putting the Artemis Accords into practice with our Moon Base. We are creating opportunities for all Artemis Accords signatories, including Paraguay, to join us on the lunar surface and advance our shared objectives in this next era of exploration.”

U.S. Embassy Asunción Chargé d’Affaires ad interim Aaron Pratt shared Isaacman’s remarks during the ceremony. Minister President of the Paraguayan Space Agency Osvaldo Almirón Riveros signed on behalf of Paraguay.

“The signing of the Artemis Accords represents a historic milestone for Paraguay and reflects our commitment to international cooperation, the peaceful use of outer space, scientific development, and the advancement of national space capabilities,” said Almirón Riveros. “This step strengthens Paraguay’s position within the global space community and opens new opportunities for research, innovation, and sustainable development.”

The Paraguayan Space Agency was established in 2014 and has worked to develop capabilities in satellite technology and Earth observation, including with international partners. Its first satellite, GuaraníSat‑1, launched from the International Space Station in 2021. The agency now is preparing to launch its second satellite, GuaraníSat‑2, in October aboard a Falcon 9 from Vandenberg Space Force Base in California. The mission was developed with collaborators from NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory and other partners.

In 2020, the United States, led by NASA and the U.S. State Department, joined with seven other founding nations to establish the Artemis Accords, responding to the growing interest in lunar activities by both governments and private companies. The Artemis Accords introduced the first set of practical principles aimed at enhancing the safety and coordination between like-minded nations as they explore the Moon, Mars, and beyond.

Signing the Artemis Accords means committing to explore peaceably and transparently, to render aid to those in need, to enable access to scientific data that all of humanity can learn from, to ensure activities do not interfere with those of others, and to preserve historically significant sites and artifacts by developing best practices for space exploration for the benefit of all.

More countries are expected to sign the Artemis Accords in the months and years ahead, as NASA continues its work to establish a safe, peaceful, and prosperous future in space.

For more information about the Artemis Accords, visit:

https://www.nasa.gov/artemis-accords
articles.reasoning: The article reports the Republic of Paraguay joining the Artemis Accords, a significant international agreement promoting peaceful, transparent, and cooperative space exploration. This event has broad significance as it expands global collaboration in space exploration, which benefits humanity by fostering scientific development, innovation, and sustainable space activities. The article provides clear context about the Artemis Accords' principles and Paraguay's growing space capabilities, making the story substantive and impactful.
articles.personality_title: Paraguay joins Artemis Accords to advance peaceful space exploration
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Artemis Accords are an international agreement that sets rules for how countries should explore space peacefully and responsibly. They were created by NASA and the U.S. government in 2020 to guide activities on the Moon, Mars, and beyond.

**What happened** – On Thursday, Paraguay officially signed the Artemis Accords during a ceremony in its capital, Asunción. This made Paraguay the 67th country to agree to follow these shared space exploration principles. The signing was done by Osvaldo Almirón Riveros, head of the Paraguayan Space Agency.

**Impact** – Paraguay’s signing shows its growing role in space science. The country has launched its first satellite and is preparing to launch a second with help from NASA. By joining the Accords, Paraguay commits to exploring space peacefully, sharing scientific data, and respecting important sites in space. This strengthens global cooperation and opens new chances for Paraguay to work with other countries on space missions.

**What's next step** – Paraguay will continue building its space programs, including launching more satellites. NASA expects more countries to join the Artemis Accords, creating a larger team working together for safe and responsible space exploration.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Paraguay’s agreement to the Artemis Accords marks a key step in expanding peaceful and cooperative space exploration worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Paraguay rejoint les Artemis Accords pour promouvoir l’exploration pacifique de l’espace
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les Artemis Accords sont un accord international qui établit des règles pour que les pays explorent l’espace de manière pacifique et responsable. Ils ont été créés par la NASA et le gouvernement américain en 2020 pour guider les activités sur la Lune, Mars et au-delà.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Jeudi, le Paraguay a officiellement signé les Artemis Accords lors d’une cérémonie dans sa capitale, Asunción. Cela fait du Paraguay le 67e pays à accepter de suivre ces principes communs d’exploration spatiale. La signature a été réalisée par Osvaldo Almirón Riveros, chef de l’Agence spatiale paraguayenne.

**Impact** – La signature du Paraguay montre son rôle croissant dans la science spatiale. Le pays a lancé son premier satellite et prépare le lancement d’un second avec l’aide de la NASA. En rejoignant les Accords, le Paraguay s’engage à explorer l’espace pacifiquement, à partager les données scientifiques et à respecter les sites importants dans l’espace. Cela renforce la coopération mondiale et ouvre de nouvelles opportunités pour le Paraguay de collaborer avec d’autres pays.

**Prochaine étape** – Le Paraguay continuera de développer ses programmes spatiaux, notamment en lançant plus de satellites. La NASA prévoit que d’autres pays rejoindront les Artemis Accords, formant une équipe plus large travaillant ensemble pour une exploration spatiale sûre et responsable.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’adhésion du Paraguay aux Artemis Accords marque une étape importante dans l’expansion d’une exploration spatiale pacifique et coopérative à l’échelle mondiale.
articles.personality_title_es: Paraguay se une a los Acuerdos Artemis para avanzar en la exploración pacífica del espacio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los Acuerdos Artemis son un acuerdo internacional que establece reglas para que los países exploren el espacio de manera pacífica y responsable. Fueron creados por la NASA y el gobierno de Estados Unidos en 2020 para guiar las actividades en la Luna, Marte y más allá.

**Qué pasó** – El jueves, Paraguay firmó oficialmente los Acuerdos Artemis durante una ceremonia en su capital, Asunción. Esto convierte a Paraguay en el país número 67 que acepta seguir estos principios compartidos para la exploración espacial. La firma fue realizada por Osvaldo Almirón Riveros, director de la Agencia Espacial Paraguaya.

**Impacto** – La firma de Paraguay muestra su papel creciente en la ciencia espacial. El país ha lanzado su primer satélite y se prepara para lanzar un segundo con ayuda de la NASA. Al unirse a los Acuerdos, Paraguay se compromete a explorar el espacio pacíficamente, compartir datos científicos y respetar sitios importantes en el espacio. Esto fortalece la cooperación global y abre nuevas oportunidades para que Paraguay colabore con otros países.

**Próximo paso** – Paraguay continuará desarrollando sus programas espaciales, incluyendo el lanzamiento de más satélites. La NASA espera que más países se unan a los Acuerdos Artemis, formando un equipo más grande que trabaje junto para una exploración espacial segura y responsable.

**Resumen en una frase** – La adhesión de Paraguay a los Acuerdos Artemis marca un paso clave para ampliar la exploración espacial pacífica y cooperativa en todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a stylized lunar landscape where a symbolic Paraguayan harp-shaped satellite gently orbits the Moon beneath a starry sky, with a diverse constellation of 67 softly glowing stars forming a protective circle around the scene, representing international unity and peaceful space exploration.
